critic_raw:
This design compares a multi-spectral CNN against an RGB-only CNN for predicting algal bloom biomass from satellite imagery over freshwater lakes.

The split is by year (train: years 1–3; val: year 4; test: year 5) but both models see all lakes in all years. Across 8–12 lakes over 5 years, the test set (year 5, all lakes) contains images from the same lakes as the training set. Because algal bloom dynamics are lake-specific (they depend on the lake's trophic state, depth, morphology, catchment characteristics), a model trained on years 1–3 of Lake A and tested on year 5 of Lake A is not performing a generalization test — it is performing within-lake extrapolation. The claimed diversity benefit ('diverse lake environments') of having 8–12 lakes is substantially weaker than it appears because both models can learn lake-specific spectral signatures from training data and apply them to test-year images from those same lakes.

The design explicitly prohibits hand-crafted spectral indices: 'No hand-crafted spectral indices (NDVI, NDCI, etc.) are computed a priori; the CNN learns spectral relationships from raw bands.' The RGB baseline also receives raw bands without spectral indices. But the multi-spectral advantage over RGB is precisely the availability of near-infrared, shortwave-infrared, and red-edge bands that the established chlorophyll indices (NDCI = (ρNIR - ρRed) / (ρNIR + ρRed), for instance) are constructed from. By forcing both models to learn these relationships from scratch rather than providing established indices, the design may inadvertently require more training data for the CNN to learn spectral-index-like combinations, and the RGB baseline is artificially penalized by receiving only three bands without the indices that would allow it to approximate chlorophyll sensitivity. This is not a test of whether multi-spectral information helps — it is a test of whether a CNN can learn multi-spectral index combinations from scratch given 3,000–5,000 image-label pairs.

In-situ sampling occurs within ±2 days of image acquisition. Algal bloom biomass can change substantially in 48 hours under favorable growth conditions. The ±2-day tolerance introduces label noise that is not symmetric: rapid bloom growth means images taken 2 days before sampling will show less biomass than the label indicates, while images taken 2 days after sampling will show more. This systematic noise is heteroscedastic — it is worse during rapid growth periods, which are precisely the high-biomass events the stakeholders most care about accurately predicting.
all_issues_raised: The temporal split by year while retaining all lakes in all years means test-set lakes are the same lakes as training-set lakes; the CNN can learn lake-specific spectral signatures and apply them to test-year images, undermining the claimed generalization test.

Prohibiting spectral indices forces both models to learn established relationships (NDCI, NDVI) from scratch; the RGB model is more disadvantaged by this choice because it lacks the bands needed to approximate chlorophyll sensitivity, making the comparison partially an artifact of the index-exclusion decision.

In-situ sampling within ±2 days introduces systematic label noise during rapid bloom growth: images taken before peak bloom underestimate biomass, and this heteroscedastic noise is worst during the high-biomass events most critical to the stakeholder application.
all_issues_adjudicated: The temporal split by year while retaining all lakes in all years means test-set lakes are the same lakes as training-set lakes; the CNN can learn lake-specific spectral signatures and apply them to test-year images, undermining the claimed generalization test.

Prohibiting spectral indices forces both models to learn established relationships (NDCI, NDVI) from scratch; the RGB model is more disadvantaged by this choice because it lacks the bands needed to approximate chlorophyll sensitivity, making the comparison partially an artifact of the index-exclusion decision.

In-situ sampling within ±2 days introduces systematic label noise during rapid bloom growth: images taken before peak bloom underestimate biomass, and this heteroscedastic noise is worst during the high-biomass events most critical to the stakeholder application.
